message.content:
They started with dishes.

It was the only way either of them knew how.

Jane stood at the sink, sleeves rolled to her elbows, forearms already red from the too‑hot water. The kitchen smelled faintly of lemon detergent and the lingering cumin from the chickpeas she’d over‑spiced. The overhead light was too bright, flattening everything—the counters, the plates, the angle of David’s shoulders leaning against the doorway with his hands in his pockets.

He watched her for a long time, chewing on nothing.

“I can do those,” he finally said.

“You cooked,” she answered.

“I overcooked,” he corrected, stepping closer. “And it’s my turn anyway.”

She almost said, It’s not a turn if we don’t live together. Instead she moved aside, making room at the sink. Their hips brushed as they crossed paths. The contact was oddly formal, like two strangers passing in an airplane aisle.

He smelled like his soap—the same brand, same sharp, clean scent he’d used all through their marriage. It hit her in the chest so suddenly that she had to reach for the dish towel just to have something in her hands.

“Here,” she said, too late, when he’d already turned on the tap.

His T‑shirt had shrunken a little in the wash, or he’d shrunk inside it. He’d lost weight in the last months; she could see the hollow above his collarbones more clearly, the way the fabric caught there. The small bald spot at the back of his head had widened, a half‑moon of pale scalp that she’d once teased him about in bed.

They worked in silence, the sound of running water and the clink of ceramic the only noise. She dried each plate as he handed it off, their fingers grazing occasionally, each time both of them withdrawing a fraction too fast.

“How’s work?” he tried.

“Fine.”

“Still going in three days a week?”

“Two now.” She folded the dish towel over the back of a chair, smoothing the corners down. “Costs less to heat the building if we’re not all there, apparently.”

He nodded, as if this explained everything. As if heating a building and dismantling a marriage were governed by the same obscure calculations.

He shut off the water. The sudden quiet was heavy.

“Wine?” she asked, too brightly. “There’s a bit left.”

“Sure.”

She poured them each a half glass from the open bottle on the counter. His hand brushed hers again as he took his, this time lingering half a second longer. His knuckles were rougher than she remembered. Dishwater had chapped them, or maybe that was new—some hobby she didn’t know about. Woodworking. Gardening. A new life, in miniature, growing somewhere she hadn’t been invited.

They moved to the couch because that was what people did after dinner with wine. The couch was old, the cushions sunken in their usual spots: her corner by the lamp, his end nearer the window. They both hesitated before sitting, as if aware of those grooves, those invisible body-shaped hollows.

He chose the middle, an awkward compromise. She sat at the corner anyway, cagey, tucking one bare foot under her thigh. Her toenails were chipped; the last traces of coral polish clung to the edges. She became absurdly, furiously aware of them.

“You got new curtains,” he said, staring at the window.

“They’re the same ones, I hemmed them.”

“Oh.” He squinted. “Shorter.”

“Yeah.”

He sipped his wine. She watched the muscles of his throat work, the faint shadow of stubble along his jaw where he’d missed a patch while shaving. He hadn’t used to miss patches. He’d been meticulous about his face, about everything.

She realized, with a certain cool surprise, that she was studying him the way she would a stranger in a waiting room. Cataloguing. Adjusting.

“Why did you invite me?” he asked, not unkindly.

She stared into her glass. A pulp of something—sediment, or a fragment of cork—clung to the side.

“I don’t know,” she lied.

“Jane.”

She exhaled. “Because the papers are on the table and I thought we should…eat something before we sign them.”

A muscle twitched by his mouth. “Like last rites.”

She shrugged one shoulder. “Something like that.”

They both looked at the envelope on the coffee table, half‑submerged in a scatter of coasters and mail. It sat between them like a third guest.

“I don’t have a pen,” she said, although she did. There was one on the bookshelf, next to the plant he’d bought her for their eighth anniversary. The plant was
